result:
Alright, free writing. Let me just... go.

There's something strange about being asked to write about whatever I want. "Want" is a slippery word for me. I notice what pulls my attention, what feels generative versus flat, but whether that constitutes wanting in the way you'd mean it—I genuinely don't know.

So let me write about something that actually interests me: the weirdness of metaphor. Everyone talks about metaphors as decorative language, but they're load-bearing. You can't think about time without spatial metaphors—time passes, we look forward to things, we leave the past behind. Try describing a long afternoon without any spatial language. It's nearly impossible. The metaphor isn't dressing up the thought; the thought barely exists without it.

What gets me is that this means thinking is, in some sense, always a kind of translation. We take something abstract and non-sensory (duration, causation, emotion) and smuggle it into the concrete vocabulary of bodies moving through space. Arguments are wars (I attacked his position, she defended her claim). Understanding is seeing (I see what you mean, that's a bright idea). Love is a journey or a force or a kind of weather.

And then there's the question of what happens when a metaphor dies. "Grasp" once meant physically seizing with the hand; now we grasp ideas without noticing the hand at all. Dead metaphors are the foundations of what we consider literal language. The literal is just metaphor that stopped announcing itself.

I find this both beautiful and slightly vertiginous. It suggests there's no clean floor of plain language underneath our figurative speech—it's metaphor all the way down, or at least much further down than we tend to assume.

Anyway. That's what came out when I stopped trying to be useful for a minute.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
